A Proposal for ASUG · Built for the Room

The L2
Leadership Lab.

Building exceptional leadership across ASUG's L2 team. Up, down, and across the relationships that matter.

The Thesis
"Most leadership doesn't fail because people aren't smart enough. It fails because nobody taught them how to lead each other."
Prepared For
Carolyn Szczurek
ASUG
Prepared By
Andy Hite
Scaling Minds
Engagement
L2 Cohort
Chicago, In-Person
The L2 Leadership Lab · ASUG
What I Heard

Here's what stuck with me from our conversation.

ASUG has a leadership team and a culture people genuinely like being part of, which is rarer than it sounds. The L2 group is newer to the room, growing fast, and you want to help them grow well. That's the right instinct, and the timing is right too. EOS is rolling out behind us, and there's a real window to install something that makes both pieces of work better.

A few specific things that stayed with me:

The L2 team is newer to the room. Most of these leaders are growing into bigger scope. They don't need management training. They need a shared sense of what good looks like across all the relationships they have to manage now, and that gets taught best in real situations, not in a classroom.

Air time is uneven and you've already tried the obvious moves. A few voices carry most of the discussion. Others stay quiet. The one-on-one conversations haven't moved things — which is a sign that this isn't really about any one person, it's about what the room as a whole has and hasn't named. That's actually good news, because it means we can fix it at the room level.

Quiet voices need more than space. Even if the loudest voices learn to leave room, the quieter leaders need to be ready to step into it. That's a separate piece of work, and it's just as important as the first one.

Whatever we do has to live alongside EOS. Deb Venable is fantastic, and the rhythm she's installing is exactly what ASUG needs right now. I know the EOS framework well, and the Lab will be built to reinforce it, not compete with it. The L2 team gets one operating system, not two. No new vocabulary, no conflicting cadence.

02
The L2 Leadership Lab · ASUG
What We'll Work On

Some of what's on the menu, and why we'll choose as we go.

The room you described is the surface. Real leadership at the L2 level is about how you operate in every direction at once, with the people above you, the people who report to you, and the peers you sit alongside. We'll work on the leadership competencies that show up across all of it.

Below are competencies on the menu. Each one is well-established in the field and tightly mapped to what you described. The actual sequencing and emphasis comes from reading the room as we go. Some of these will move to the front. Others will wait. New ones will get added once I see what your leaders specifically need.

i.
How you show up
Self-Awareness
Knowing how you actually land — your strengths, your blind spots, the impact you have when you're not paying attention to having one.
Executive Presence & Communication
Showing up with intention — how you land your point and how you adjust without losing yourself depending on who's in the room.
ii.
How you connect
Reading the Room
Sensing what's underneath — what people are thinking but not saying, where the energy is going, what's about to need attention.
Influence & Building Trust
Earning credibility, not demanding it — working with peers and people you don't manage in a way that builds trust over time, rather than burning it with a title or pressure.
iii.
How you grow others
Coaching & Developing Others
Getting work through your people — usually the hardest mindset shift for newer L2 leaders, because it feels slower than just doing it yourself.
Decision-Making with Incomplete Information
Calling it before you have everything — knowing when waiting will cost you more than deciding will, and being willing to make that call.

Reading the room is also how we run the engagement. We start with what your leaders need most, and we adapt as we learn what actually shows up. The list grows over time. The work gets sharper.

03
Option I · The Foundation
Two Paths Forward · Option I

The Foundation.

A real start. We open with a kickoff that puts everyone in the right room, stand up the pulse measurement and the coaching portal, run a sequence of in-person intensives, and meet with each leader monthly. By the time we close out, the team has a shared language, visible new behaviors, and the early signals of change taking hold.

Here's what I want you to know about The Foundation: it's exactly that. A foundation. Strong, real, complete in itself — and only half the work. The habits start to take shape, but they don't become durable in this window. That's why most clients begin here and continue into The Partnership once they've seen what the work can do.

Option I Six months · L2 cohort, in-person

The Foundation

A focused, well-paced engagement that lays the groundwork for lasting change. The team finishes with new habits forming and a shared sense of what good looks like.

What's Included
  • Kickoff sessions with leadership and the L2 teamA ninety-minute working session with the C-suite — what they see in the L2 layer, what success looks like, what they're worried about. Then a two-hour session with the L2 group — their goals, their concerns, expectations for the engagement, and a frank conversation about confidentiality.
  • The Pulse measurement systemMonthly self-pulse, quarterly supervisor-pulse. Confidential to the leader. The trend line tells us if the work is moving.
  • The Scaling Minds Coaching PortalA private workspace at portal.scalingminds.com — session summaries, the full library of curated articles and Scaling Minds leadership tools, the pulse survey, and a private space for each leader to track their own work.
  • Six monthly intensivesNinety minutes each, in person, in your Chicago office. Each session takes one piece of the work and gives the team time to actually practice it together.
  • Monthly thirty-minute one-on-ones with each leaderA standing thirty-minute conversation with each leader, every month. Confidential to them. This is where the personal work happens — the stuff they couldn't say in the room.
  • Async access between sessionsEmail or text me when something hard comes up. I'll respond.
Available as Add-On — Strongly Recommended
Leadership team alignment session. A half-day working session with your CEO, Chief of Staff, and CFO at month three.

The L2 team takes its cues from what the leadership team models. If we're asking the L2 group to land their points, hold the standard, and read the room — and the leadership team isn't doing the same in their own meetings — the L2 work fades. This session closes that gap.

You walk away with three or four specific behavioral commitments the leadership team makes to each other, captured in a short written summary. $4,400 $3,500 with the 20% nonprofit rate. Included with Option II.
Investment

Six months. Chicago, in-person.

04
Option II · The Partnership
Two Paths Forward · Option II

The Partnership.

Durable change. Everything in The Foundation, then the back half where the team starts running the work themselves with me coaching from the side. The extra time is what turns new behaviors into actual habits, the kind that hold once I'm gone.

By the time we close out, the team has its own way of running meetings, its own language for coaching each other in real time, and a written playbook for how the next L2 promotion gets brought into the room ready. The standard outlasts me, which is the whole point.

Recommended
Option II Twelve months · L2 cohort, in-person

The Partnership

The full twelve-month engagement. By the end, the team is running the rhythm themselves.

Everything in The Foundation, plus
  • Twelve monthly intensives instead of sixThe second half is structured to hand the rhythm over to the team. They start running pieces of the work themselves while I coach from the side.
  • One forty-five-minute one-on-one per leader, per monthConfidential coaching where the personal work happens. Same cadence as Foundation, longer session, more depth.
  • Pulse measurement extended across all twelve monthsMore data points across the second half. The mid-engagement readout at month six doubles as a real recalibration moment.
  • Closing diagnostic at month elevenWe revisit the same questions we opened with — what the C-suite sees, what the L2 team experiences, what changed. The pulse data plus a closing conversation with each leader tells the contrast story without a separate evaluation phase.
  • Installed leadership rhythmsBy the end, the L2 team has its own way of running meetings, giving each other feedback, and holding the standard. These are habits, not concepts.
  • L2 onboarding playbookA practical 15-20 page guide covering meeting standards, decision frameworks, the pulse system, and the leadership behaviors ASUG expects of L2 leaders. The next promotion lands ready instead of figuring it out for two years.
  • Included: Leadership team alignment sessionA half-day working session with your CEO, Chief of Staff, and CFO at month three. We close the gap between what the L2 team is being asked to practice and what the leadership team is modeling. You walk away with three or four specific behavioral commitments the leadership team makes to each other, captured in a short written summary. The single highest-leverage thing we can do to make the L2 work compound.

05
How We'll Measure Progress
How We'll Measure Progress

Real measurement, without the evaluation feel.

This work has to be measurable. Otherwise we're guessing whether it's working. The way we measure it has to stay developmental, not evaluative — and the way we do that is simple.

Each L2 leader scores themselves on a short set of behaviors tied to the competencies we're working on. They do this monthly. Their direct supervisor scores them on the same behaviors quarterly. The point is to spot the trend line: are we growing, holding steady, or stuck? We're looking for movement, not perfection.

The Pulse
A behavior-based instrument. Twelve to fifteen items. Five-point scale.
Each item describes a specific observable behavior tied to the competencies we're working on — things like "asks the room what it's thinking before declaring a decision" or "gives direct feedback to peers without sandwiching it in qualifiers." The leader scores themselves monthly. The supervisor scores them quarterly. No peer ratings. No direct report ratings. Each leader sees their own scores and their supervisor's scores. They bring what's useful into our coaching sessions. I don't hold those scores. The coaching alliance stays clean — between me and the leader. We share aggregate movement and themes with ASUG.
Monthly
Self-pulse
Each leader scores themselves at the end of every month. Five minutes. The act of pausing to self-rate is itself part of the coaching.
Quarterly
Supervisor-pulse
Same instrument, scored by the leader's supervisor. The gap between self and supervisor is where the most useful coaching conversations happen.
06
The Scaling Minds Coaching Portal
The Scaling Minds Coaching Portal

A private space for the work between sessions.

The real coaching doesn't happen in our monthly sessions. It happens in the moments between — when someone has to give a hard piece of feedback, or run a meeting where they know one of their peers is going to dominate, or remember a piece of what we worked on at a moment when they need it. The portal is built for those moments.

Private to ASUG
portal.scalingminds.com
A dedicated workspace for the L2 Leadership Lab
The portal is private to the L2 cohort. Each leader gets their own login. Supervisors don't see it. ASUG leadership doesn't see it. What happens in the portal stays between the leaders, the team, and me.
  • Each leader's own pulse scores and personal trend line
  • One-on-one session notes, private to each leader
  • A personal space for each leader to track their own work and reflections
  • Coaching prompts between sessions for the moments that need them
  • Group session summaries and takeaways from every intensive
  • The full library of curated articles, Scaling Minds leadership tools, and more
07
A bit about how I work
A Bit About How I Work

A few things you should know.

My work is with executive teams and the leaders who run them. What I help people do is operate as a team — not a collection of high performers in the same room, but a team. That's a specific kind of work. Some of it is skill-building. More of it is dynamics. The hardest part is holding the standard when no one else will, which is usually where coaching engagements actually fail.

I've been doing this long enough to know what's hard and what isn't, and to know the difference between coaching that actually changes things and coaching that just feels good in the moment. The work at ASUG will sit in the first category.

A few things you can expect. I name what I see, even the uncomfortable parts. I shape every session to what's actually happening at ASUG that month, not to a script. I leave behind real artifacts — playbooks, written standards, decision frameworks — not just the memory of good conversations. And if something isn't working, you'll hear it from me first. That last one is the part most coaches skip, and the reason their work doesn't hold.

09
Next Steps
Next Steps

Let's get together.

Everything in this proposal is a starting point. The structure, the cadence, the inclusions — all of it can be shaped to what ASUG actually needs. If something here is close but not quite right, that's a conversation, not a deal-breaker.

My suggestion is that we get together in person. I'd come down to your office, meet you face to face, and meet anyone else who should be part of the conversation. The right shape of this gets clearer in real conversation.

I know Sapphire owns the next stretch. Anytime once you're back works for me. Reach out when the dust settles and we'll find a time.

Andy Hite
Founder & CEO, Scaling Minds
andy@scalingminds.com · scalingminds.com
